video_title: Katrina: Come Hell and High Water | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I get the need to point out the issues of Katrina on NO, but there needs to be a bit of reality to the NO perspective.  NO wasn't a city to raise a family in before Katrina for a good portion of the city and while coverage and carpet bagging definitely has happened, too many of the people in the documentary paint this almost rosie picture of NO pre-Katrina.  I'm from Puerto Rico and the people there are the same way.  Valuing identity above economic improvement does nothing.  The whole "gentrification" complaint while having stories where you want people to see the value in NO are very contradicting.  Nobody's going to invest in a place and want to somehow make a place affordable to people that admittedly were unable to afford the cheap property in pre-Katrina NO, much less new homes in a much-improved area.  People hate change.  Especially change that crushes those of us that have little or little power to do anything.  But none of that is new.  Banks have literally killed most neighborhoods in the country, and the documentary gives the impression that NO is somehow having a unique experience in that regard.  I think the documentary would've been better had it focused on the parts of Katrina that were truly tragic.  There is no reason to treat Americans the way Americans were treated in NO.  NO shares a lot with Puerto Rico.  A beautiful city, beautiful people, great culture.....and no room for what makes it great, the people.  That's the reality, that whether you're a native of NO, or a Puerto Rican living on the island, if you're of modest income or less, you're invisible.  You're seen as an albatross that's in the way of progress.  You're taken advantage of, pushed aside.  That's what's happening everywhere and the reality is that most middle-class types don't realize they are included with us.  That's the knowledge that can be used to strengthen resolve to self-improve.  Instead of absorbing what the documentary tries to sell as reality (that investment in NO is a bad thing if it means gentrification), people in NO need to position themselves to be in an opportunity stream.  I get that a ton of people were forced to leave, but plenty stayed behind and thrived, not just the high-profile types that were in the documentary.

video_title: Katrina: Come Hell and High Water | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
This whole situation with Hurricane Katrina will never sit right with me. People weren’t “looting” for greed, they were in survival mode—starving, thirsty, trying to keep their families alive. Instead of compassion, you had armed officers shooting at civilians who just wanted to live. And what makes it worse is that reporters were there filming the suffering, yet aid was nowhere to be found. It became painfully obvious that race, status, and money decided who got saved. If you were wealthy, you had options. If not, you were abandoned. Truly heartbreaking and unjust.


When Hurricane Katrina happened, I was only 8 years old and living on the East Coast, so I didn’t fully understand it at the time. But now that I’m older, I realize just how severe this disaster was. Those poor families, the kids sitting in the heat, the animals left behind—it’s heartbreaking. People were basically left to die, and the government completely failed them. Truly one of the most tragic moments in history.

The place i stayed in got hit by a tree and the shrapnel from the sheetrock hit me in the head and the inlet lights started pouring water I just sat their in complete shock. I remember the sound of generators for miles and hearing about people looting in the neighborhoods. Couldnt sleep at night much you had to keep an eye out for looters was afraid someone was going to come up on us at night while sleeping. It got super hot too i slept next to my aquarium at the time to keep it alive i built a solar generator when katrina hit for it to survive. So i would sleep next to it to make sure the pumps stayed on for my fish i didnt want to suffocate from the lack of power. There were people who swam out of the 2nd floor of their homes in the neighborhood i was in. I remember hearing on the radio people calling in trapped in their attics because of the water coming up. Hurricane Katrina hit on my 19th birthday on the year i graduated.

comment_text: I was a freshman at Dillard University and we evacuated Sat evening to Shreveport,  while my bff was at Xavier - they stayed behind. She was stuck there and on a roof top waiting for rescue. Being from California we had never experienced anything like this before. My cousin and her bf ended up in Houston with his family. There was no communication - phones would say all circuits busy so we had to rely on what the news reported to find people. It was traumatizing and just sad. I love New Orleans, the spirit of the people, the culture. It took me a long time to make it back to visit and it was not the same. Never forgotten❤

comment_text: I was in the navy at the time. Stationed in Norfolk. A few days after Katrina, I was aboard a military transport plane flying to the Navy base in New Orleans. Some of the cooks were stationed in New Orleans and had last their homes or apartments, but were still there helping. I was down there for a month before going back to my homebase of Norfolk. Can't believe it's been 20 years.

comment_text: It's not looting if it's food, water, and toiletries. That's survival. After Beryl here in Houston, the cops were down by Hiram Clarke CONSTANTLY even tho we didn't have any where to loot from... and they didn't help us when the power was out for days and the heat was rising, they were just there to intimidate... our tire blew due to the debris around and we tried to flag down a cop, because the wheel was tightened beyond what two hungry, tired, and exhausted people could undo and they drove passed us to get food at the jack in the box... Then when we got robbed, they took 2 hours to show up and closed that case without ever coming back...
